claims.claim: Whole-prompt similarity metrics conflate semantic relatedness with exact prefix overlap, yielding both missed reuse and high false-positive rates.

evidence_refs.quote: # Reuse-Fingerprint Student: A Compact Fingerprint-Pair Admission Policy for Exact Prefix-Cache Reuse

claims.notes: Historical backfill linked the claim to already published artifacts; this is not independent source revalidation.

claims.claim: The student is trained to approximate an oracle that computes the true longest common prefix (LCP) between each incoming request and every slot in a recent-cache window, labeling a pair as reusable when the LCP meets or exceeds a threshold of 64 exact prefix tokens (organized as 16-token blocks × 4).

evidence_refs.quote: # Reuse-Fingerprint Student: A Compact Fingerprint-Pair Admission Policy for Exact Prefix-Cache Reuse

claims.notes: Historical backfill linked the claim to already published artifacts; this is not independent source revalidation.

claims.claim: The central question is whether this compact feature set suffices to recover oracle-level reuse capture while eliminating the false-positive admissions produced by simpler baselines, or whether the problem requires richer and more expensive comparison.

evidence_refs.quote: # Reuse-Fingerprint Student: A Compact Fingerprint-Pair Admission Policy for Exact Prefix-Cache Reuse

claims.notes: Historical backfill linked the claim to already published artifacts; this is not independent source revalidation.

claims.claim: It is trained on pair-level labels derived from the oracle LCP: a pair is positive if the true LCP is ≥ 64 tokens (4 blocks × 16 tokens/block).
